perturbation.original_text: VENDOR shall maintain the privacy of protected personal information and shall be financially responsible, if and to the extent that any security breach relating to protected personal information results from acts or omissions of VENDOR, or its personnel, for any notifications to affected persons (after prompt consultation with UNIVERSITY), and to the extent requested by UNIVERSITY, administratively responsible for such notification.
perturbation.changed_text: VENDOR shall endeavor to maintain the privacy of protected personal information and will discuss financial responsibility on a case-by-case basis, if and to the extent that any security breach relating to protected personal information results from acts or omissions of VENDOR, or its personnel, for any notifications to affected persons (after prompt consultation with UNIVERSITY), and to the extent requested by UNIVERSITY, administratively responsible for such notification.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall maintain' to 'shall endeavor to maintain' and removing the direct financial responsibility creates ambiguity. This contradicts the implicit assumption of strict liability for security breaches stated in the previous sentence and undermines the UNIVERSITY's confidence in the security and financial safeguards, as mentioned in section 1 where the UNIVERSITY's willingness to disclose Confidential Information hinges on VENDOR's commitment to the agreement's terms and privacy laws.
perturbation.contradicted_text: VENDOR further acknowledges that UNIVERSITY’S willingness to disclose Confidential Information to VENDOR is predicated on VENDOR entering into committing to abide by the terms of this Agreement and all applicable privacy laws.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated that the VENDOR 'shall maintain' privacy and 'shall be financially responsible'. The changed text weakens both obligations to 'shall endeavor to maintain' and 'will discuss financial responsibility on a case-by-case basis'. This introduces ambiguity regarding the VENDOR's commitment to data privacy and financial accountability in case of a breach, creating a direct contradiction with the 'Contradicted Text,' which states that the UNIVERSITY'S willingness to disclose confidential information is predicated on the VENDOR committing to abiding by the terms of the agreement. The original text's stronger guarantees are replaced with weaker, less certain obligations, thus creating an Ambiguity - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: VENDOR shall immediately notify UNIVERSITY in writing of any subpoena, court order, or other legal process seeking or purporting to compel disclosure of any of the Confidential Information and shall challenge, oppose, or appeal any such subpoena, order, or legal process to the extent deemed appropriate by UNIVERSITY.
perturbation.changed_text: VENDOR shall notify UNIVERSITY in writing within five business days of any subpoena, court order, or other legal process seeking or purporting to compel disclosure of any of the Confidential Information and may challenge, oppose, or appeal any such subpoena, order, or legal process to the extent deemed appropriate by VENDOR.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'immediately notify' to 'notify within five business days' introduces a delay and changing 'shall challenge' to 'may challenge' gives VENDOR discretion. This contradicts the requirement for VENDOR to use 'best efforts at all times to adopt and follow procedures and practices to protect the confidentiality of the Confidential Information' mentioned in section 1, creating uncertainty about the confidentiality of information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: VENDOR, its employees, agents, contractors and subcontractors may, in addition to their respective obligations hereunder, use reasonable efforts at all times to adopt and follow procedures and practices to protect the confidentiality of the Confidential Information and prevent its disclosure to others without the express written consent of UNIVERSITY. VENDOR specifically, acknowledges Federal privacy laws such as Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act (Title 15, United States Code, Sections 6801(b) and 6805(b)(2)) applicable to financial transactions and Family Educational Rights and P r i v a c y A c t ( T i t l e 2 0 , U n i t e d S t a t e s C o d e , S e c t i o n 1 2 3 2 g ) a p p l i c a b l e t o student records and information from student records. VENDOR further acknowledges that UNIVERSITY’S willingness to disclose Confidential Information to VENDOR is predicated on VENDOR entering into committing to abide by the terms of this Agreement and all applicable privacy laws.
perturbation.justification: The original text required VENDOR to 'immediately notify' and 'shall challenge,' ensuring swift action and minimal discretion. The changed text allows for notification within 'five business days' and gives VENDOR the option to 'may challenge.' This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states VENDOR, its employees, agents, contractors and subcontractors may, in addition to their respective obligations hereunder, use reasonable efforts at all times to adopt and follow procedures and practices to protect the confidentiality of the Confidential Information and prevent its disclosure to others without the express written consent of UNIVERSITY, because giving VENDOR the option to challenge a subpoena and delaying notification introduces a risk of disclosure which the VENDOR is required to protect according to the contradicted text.

perturbation.original_text: Upon request of UNIVERSITY at any time VENDOR shall return all or such part of the Confidential Information as UNIVERSITY may designate to be returned. In addition, upon the completion of the services provided by VENDOR to UNIVERSITY, VENDOR shall return or destroy, as UNIVERSITY may instruct, all Confidential Information in VENDOR’S possession or control, whether in printed, electronic or any other format, including all duplicates and copies thereof of any files, compilation, study, report, analysis, or data base containing, based on or derived from the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon reasonable request of UNIVERSITY, VENDOR shall make good faith efforts to return most of the Confidential Information as UNIVERSITY may designate to be returned. In addition, upon the completion of the services provided by VENDOR to UNIVERSITY, VENDOR shall return or endeavor to destroy, as UNIVERSITY may reasonably instruct, a substantial portion of Confidential Information in VENDOR’S possession or control, whether in printed, electronic or any other format, including all duplicates and copies thereof of any files, compilation, study, report, analysis, or data base containing, based on or derived from the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'reasonable request', changing 'shall return all' to 'make good faith efforts to return most', and changing 'shall destroy' to 'endeavor to destroy a substantial portion' weakens the obligation to return and destroy all confidential information. This directly contradicts the purpose of the agreement and introduces ambiguity concerning the completeness of information control and the level of confidentiality assurance offered to the UNIVERSITY as expressed in the preamble.
perturbation.contradicted_text: WHEREAS, the parties wish to confirm their understandings with respect to the confidential nature of the relationship between the parties relating to the transmission, use, and protection of certain confidential data, it is hereby agreed as follows: 1. Confidential Information. VENDOR acknowledges that in the course of providing services to UNIVERSITY, VENDOR will be given or have access to confidential student and/or employee information (“Confidential Information”) protected from disclosure by privacy laws. VENDOR, its employees, agents, contractors and subcontractors may, in addition to their respective obligations hereunder, use reasonable efforts at all times to adopt and follow procedures and practices to protect the confidentiality of the Confidential Information and prevent its disclosure to others without the express written consent of UNIVERSITY. VENDOR specifically, acknowledges Federal privacy laws such as Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act (Title 15, United States Code, Sections 6801(b) and 6805(b)(2)) applicable to financial transactions and Family Educational Rights and P r i v a c y A c t ( T i t l e 2 0 , U n i t e d S t a t e s C o d e , S e c t i o n 1 2 3 2 g ) a p p l i c a b l e t o student records and information from student records. VENDOR further acknowledges that UNIVERSITY’S willingness to disclose Confidential Information to VENDOR is predicated on VENDOR entering into committing to abide by the terms of this Agreement and all applicable privacy laws.
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the obligation to return and destroy confidential information by adding 'reasonable request', changing 'shall return all' to 'make good faith efforts to return most', and changing 'shall destroy' to 'endeavor to destroy a substantial portion'. This directly contradicts the purpose of the agreement and introduces ambiguity concerning the completeness of information control and the level of confidentiality assurance offered to the UNIVERSITY as expressed in the contradicted text, which emphasizes the confidential nature of the relationship and the protection of confidential data.

perturbation.original_text: VENDOR’S disclosure of Confidential Information to third parties without UNIVERSITY’S authorization, or VENDOR’S failure to return or destroy Confidential Information per Provision 4, above may result in the UNIVERSITY or the U.S. Department of Education denying VENDOR access to Confidential Information for a period of at least five years.
perturbation.changed_text: VENDOR’S intentional disclosure of Confidential Information to third parties with gross negligence and without UNIVERSITY’S authorization, or VENDOR’S significant failure to return or destroy Confidential Information per Provision 4, above may result in the UNIVERSITY considering the option of or the U.S. Department of Education potentially denying VENDOR access to Confidential Information for a period of up to five years.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'intentional disclosure with gross negligence', changing 'failure' to 'significant failure', and changing 'may result' to 'University considering the option of' and 'potentially denying' adds conditions to the consequences for disclosing or failing to return confidential information, thereby weakening the stated repercussions. By making the consequences optional and increasing the burden of proof, it directly contradicts the prior unambiguous stipulations and diminishes the overall deterrent effect, thereby contradicting the intent of the agreement as expressed in the preamble and undermining the UNIVERSITY's confidence that the vendor will be responsible with its information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: VENDOR further acknowledges that UNIVERSITY’S willingness to disclose Confidential Information to VENDOR is predicated on VENDOR entering into committing to abide by the terms of this Agreement and all applicable privacy laws.
perturbation.justification: The original text states clear consequences for the vendor's disclosure or failure to return confidential information. The changed text weakens these consequences by adding conditions like 'intentional disclosure with gross negligence' and 'significant failure,' and by making the repercussions optional ('University considering the option of,' 'potentially denying'). This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states that the university's willingness to disclose confidential information is based on the vendor committing to the terms of the agreement. By weakening the consequences and adding conditions, the changed text undermines the vendor's commitment to the terms of the agreement, thus creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: VENDOR shall give UNIVERSITY immediate notice of any unauthorized use or disclosure of the Confidential Information, or of any breach or threatened breach by VENDOR, or its employees, agents, contractors, or subcontractors of their obligations under this Agreement, upon learning of same.
perturbation.changed_text: VENDOR shall use reasonable efforts to give UNIVERSITY notice within a commercially reasonable timeframe of any known unauthorized use or disclosure of the Confidential Information, or of any known breach by VENDOR, or its employees, agents, contractors, or subcontractors of their obligations under this Agreement, promptly after becoming aware of it.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'immediate notice' to 'reasonable efforts to give notice within a commercially reasonable timeframe' and adding 'known' before 'unauthorized use or disclosure' introduces subjectivity and delay. This makes the notification obligation less strict and creates uncertainty about what constitutes a 'commercially reasonable timeframe' and what knowledge the VENDOR is reasonably expected to have. This change contradicts the intention of swift and guaranteed communication as previously established in section 3 by the 'shall immediately notify UNIVERSITY', which creates internal contradiction and impacts the UNIVERSITY'S capacity to respond adequately to data breaches.
perturbation.contradicted_text: VENDOR shall notify UNIVERSITY in writing within five business days of any subpoena, court order, or other legal process seeking or purporting to compel disclosure of any of the Confidential Information and may challenge, oppose, or appeal any such subpoena, order, or legal process to the extent deemed appropriate by VENDOR. In no event shall VENDOR voluntarily, without a court order, disclose or permit the disclosure of any of the Confidential Information in response to legal process unless and until VENDOR has given the required notice to UNIVERSITY and VENDOR has exhausted any and all legal remedies available to it to limit or prevent the disclosure.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires 'immediate notice', which is a clear and unambiguous obligation. The changed text replaces 'immediate notice' with 'reasonable efforts to give notice within a commercially reasonable timeframe' and adds 'known' before 'unauthorized use or disclosure'. This introduces ambiguity and subjectivity, making the notification obligation less strict. The contradicted text states 'VENDOR shall notify UNIVERSITY in writing within five business days', which also provides a clear timeframe for notification. The change from 'immediate' to 'commercially reasonable timeframe' creates a direct conflict with the five-day requirement, because a commercially reasonable timeframe may be longer than 5 business days. Furthermore, the addition of 'known' also introduces a degree of subjectivity that contrasts with the clear obligation of notification in the contradicted text, because it is unclear whether the VENDOR needs to actively discover the breach or simply report breaches that it is aware of. The original text and contradicted text are aiming for a swift reaction, while the changed text contradicts that.
